ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper does not introduce a fundamentally new paradigm but builds on the existing "compare-aggregate" family (e.g. Parikh et al. 2016, match-LSTM) and re-uses comparison primitives (element-wise SUB/MULT were previously used by Mou et al. 2016 and He & Lin explored cosine/Euclidean). Its main novelty is empirical: a systematic comparison of six word-level comparison functions and demonstration of a compare-aggregate variant (gate-based preprocessing, one-directional attention, CNN aggregation) across four diverse datasets, showing SUB/MULT work well. These are useful empirical findings and modest architectural tweaks, but amount to an incremental rather than a highly original contribution.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized (Intro → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and explicitly states its two main contributions early in the Introduction. Notation is largely consistent and definitions (Q, A, dimensions, comparison functions, and equations 1–9) make the method easy to follow. Presentation issues include duplicated/repeated text and a few formatting/notation ambiguities in the preprocessing equation (gate × tanh appears missing an explicit operator) and a couple of typos (e.g. “Siamense”), and figures/tables are frequently referenced but not visible in the provided text—these are minor but affect smooth readability.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear technical claims (Section 1) that a compare-aggregate architecture with element-wise comparison functions (SUB, MULT, SUBMULT+NN, defined in Section 2.3) works well across four datasets, and supports them with multi-dataset experiments and ablations (Sections 3.1–3.4). The mathematical components are simple and appear correct (preprocessing, attention in Eqn.2, comparison definitions Eqns.3–8, aggregation Eqn.9). Methodology is generally sound — multiple diverse datasets and relevant baselines are considered (Section 3.2) — but the authors rely on reported baseline numbers rather than reimplementations and report no statistical significance tests, and ablation details are limited, which are minor issues that do not invalidate the main conclusions.
